title: Countable & Uncountable Noun Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: In English, some nouns are countable (you can count them one by one - one book, two books), while others are uncountable (they describe a whole mass, substance, or idea that can't be split into single units - water, furniture, advice). Uncountable nouns never take a plural '-s', and they never take 'a/an' directly. Here are 50 of the most common mistakes with this rule, explained simply, with an easy way to fix each one.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have many furnitures in my house.
commonMistakes.right: I have a lot of furniture in my house.
commonMistakes.why: 'Furniture' is an uncountable noun - it refers to a whole category of items (tables, chairs, sofas) as one general idea, not individual countable pieces, so it never takes an 's'. To describe quantity, use 'a lot of', 'some', or 'a piece of furniture' instead of a plural ending.

commonMistakes.wrong: She gave me some advices.
commonMistakes.right: She gave me some advice.
commonMistakes.why: 'Advice' names a general kind of help or guidance, not separate countable 'advices', so English never adds 's' to it. If you want to refer to one instance, say 'a piece of advice', not 'an advice'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need some informations about the course.
commonMistakes.right: I need some information about the course.
commonMistakes.why: 'Information' describes a whole body of knowledge, not individual countable 'informations', so it stays singular with no 's'. Use 'a piece of information' or 'some information' when you want to be more specific.

commonMistakes.wrong: He gave me many feedbacks on my work.
commonMistakes.right: He gave me a lot of feedback on my work.
commonMistakes.why: 'Feedback' is treated as an uncountable mass of comments and opinions, not separate countable items, so it doesn't take a plural form. Use 'a lot of feedback' or simply 'some feedback' to express quantity.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have two luggages with me.
commonMistakes.right: I have two pieces of luggage with me.
commonMistakes.why: 'Luggage' refers to your bags and suitcases as one collective category, so it never becomes plural itself. To count individual bags, use 'a piece of luggage' or simply name the items (two bags, two suitcases).

commonMistakes.wrong: She bought new furnitures for her home.
commonMistakes.right: She bought new furniture for her home.
commonMistakes.why: As with the earlier example, 'furniture' can never be made plural, no matter how many individual items you're describing. Practice saying 'some new furniture' instead of adding an 's'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need some equipments for the lab.
commonMistakes.right: I need some equipment for the lab.
commonMistakes.why: 'Equipment' describes tools and machines collectively as one category, so it stays singular even when referring to many items. Use 'a piece of equipment' if you want to talk about just one item.

commonMistakes.wrong: He has a lot of knowledges about history.
commonMistakes.right: He has a lot of knowledge about history.
commonMistakes.why: 'Knowledge' represents an entire body of understanding, not separate countable 'knowledges', so it always stays singular. Simply say 'a lot of knowledge' or 'deep knowledge', with no 's' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: I bought some breads from the store.
commonMistakes.right: I bought some bread from the store.
commonMistakes.why: 'Bread' refers to the food as a whole substance, not individual countable units, so it doesn't take a plural form (though 'loaves of bread' can describe distinct pieces). Just say 'some bread' to describe any quantity.

commonMistakes.wrong: Can I have two waters, please?
commonMistakes.right: Can I have two glasses of water, please?
commonMistakes.why: 'Water' is an uncountable liquid, so on its own it never becomes plural - but you CAN count the containers holding it. Use 'glasses of water' or 'bottles of water' to specify amount.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has beautiful hairs.
commonMistakes.right: She has beautiful hair.
commonMistakes.why: When talking about all the hair on someone's head as a whole, 'hair' is uncountable and stays singular (only use 'hairs' when counting individual strands, like 'three gray hairs'). For describing hair in general, always use the singular form.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need some more moneys.
commonMistakes.right: I need some more money.
commonMistakes.why: 'Money' as a general concept is uncountable, so it never takes a plural 's', even though you can count individual coins or notes. Use 'some money' or 'a lot of money' to describe an amount.

commonMistakes.wrong: There was a lot of traffics on the road.
commonMistakes.right: There was a lot of traffic on the road.
commonMistakes.why: 'Traffic' describes the overall flow of vehicles as one mass concept, not individual countable 'traffics'. Always keep 'traffic' singular, no matter how heavy it is.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have too many homeworks today.
commonMistakes.right: I have too much homework today.
commonMistakes.why: 'Homework' is uncountable, so it pairs with 'much' (for uncountable nouns) rather than 'many' (for countable nouns), and it never takes an 's'. Remember this pairing rule: many + countable, much + uncountable.

commonMistakes.wrong: She gave us useful informations.
commonMistakes.right: She gave us useful information.
commonMistakes.why: As before, 'information' never takes a plural form, regardless of how many separate facts it might contain. Keep practicing this word until 'informations' stops feeling natural to write.

commonMistakes.wrong: I bought two soaps from the shop.
commonMistakes.right: I bought two bars of soap from the shop.
commonMistakes.why: 'Soap' as a substance is uncountable, so you count the containers or units it comes in (bars, bottles), not the soap itself. Say 'two bars of soap', not 'two soaps', unless referring to two different brands/types.

commonMistakes.wrong: He gave me many advices about my career.
commonMistakes.right: He gave me a lot of advice about my career.
commonMistakes.why: This is the same rule as before applied again - 'advice' is always singular, no matter how many separate pieces of guidance were given. Repetition helps: practice 'a lot of advice' until it becomes automatic.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need some new stationaries for school.
commonMistakes.right: I need some new stationery for school.
commonMistakes.why: 'Stationery' (pens, paper, and other writing supplies) is treated as one uncountable category in English, so it doesn't take a plural form. Use 'some stationery' or 'stationery items' to describe several different things.

commonMistakes.wrong: There are many furnitures missing from the room.
commonMistakes.right: There is a lot of furniture missing from the room.
commonMistakes.why: Beyond just the noun, notice the verb changes too - since 'furniture' is singular (uncountable), the verb must be 'is', not 'are'. Uncountable nouns affect both the noun form and the verb that follows.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have a work to finish today.
commonMistakes.right: I have some work to finish today.
commonMistakes.why: 'Work' meaning a task or job in general is uncountable, so it can't take 'a' directly in front of it (though 'a job' or 'a task' works fine as countable alternatives). Use 'some work' or simply 'work' with no article.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has many experiences in teaching.
commonMistakes.right: She has a lot of experience in teaching.
commonMistakes.why: When 'experience' means general knowledge gained over time, it's uncountable (though 'experiences' can be countable when referring to specific separate events, like 'she shared some experiences from her trip'). For general skill or background, keep it singular: 'a lot of experience'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need an accommodation for tonight.
commonMistakes.right: I need accommodation for tonight.
commonMistakes.why: 'Accommodation' (a place to stay) is uncountable in British English, so it doesn't take 'a/an' or a plural form. Simply say 'I need accommodation' without any article.

commonMistakes.wrong: He gave some good suggestions and advices.
commonMistakes.right: He gave some good suggestions and advice.
commonMistakes.why: Notice that 'suggestions' can correctly be plural (it's countable), but 'advice' next to it must stay singular, since the two words follow different rules. Always check each noun separately rather than assuming they behave the same way.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have less problems than before.
commonMistakes.right: I have fewer problems than before.
commonMistakes.why: Although this is a countable/uncountable-related mistake, the fix is about word choice: use 'fewer' with countable nouns (problems) and 'less' with uncountable nouns (money, time). Remember: fewer + countable, less + uncountable.

commonMistakes.wrong: There is less people at the party than expected.
commonMistakes.right: There are fewer people at the party than expected.
commonMistakes.why: 'People' is a countable plural noun, so it needs 'fewer' (not 'less') and the plural verb 'are'. This is one of the most common mistakes even among native speakers, so it's worth mastering carefully.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't have much friends here.
commonMistakes.right: I don't have many friends here.
commonMistakes.why: 'Friends' is countable, so it pairs with 'many', not 'much', which is reserved for uncountable nouns. Keep repeating the rule: many/few + countable, much/little + uncountable.

commonMistakes.wrong: She doesn't have many money.
commonMistakes.right: She doesn't have much money.
commonMistakes.why: This is the reverse mistake - 'money' is uncountable, so it needs 'much', not 'many'. Practicing both directions (many friends / much money) together helps the rule stick.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need a piece of advices.
commonMistakes.right: I need a piece of advice.
commonMistakes.why: Once you use 'a piece of' to count an uncountable noun, the noun itself must stay singular - 'a piece of advices' mixes singular and plural incorrectly. The phrase 'a piece of' already shows quantity, so the noun after it never needs its own 's'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Can you give me two informations?
commonMistakes.right: Can you give me two pieces of information?
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'advice', if you want to count 'information', use 'pieces of information', not a number directly with the uncountable noun. Never place a number directly before an uncountable noun without a counting phrase like 'piece(s) of'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I bought some new furnitures and clothes.
commonMistakes.right: I bought some new furniture and clothes.
commonMistakes.why: 'Furniture' stays singular (never 'furnitures'), while 'clothes' is a normal plural countable noun that's fine with an 's' - both fit naturally after 'some', but only one of them can take a plural ending. Check each noun in a sentence individually rather than assuming they all follow the same countable/uncountable rule.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need one equipment for this experiment.
commonMistakes.right: I need a piece of equipment for this experiment.
commonMistakes.why: You can't place 'one' directly before an uncountable noun like 'equipment' - use 'a piece of equipment' instead to count a single item. This mirrors the same fix used for advice and information.

commonMistakes.wrong: There were many thunders last night.
commonMistakes.right: There was a lot of thunder last night.
commonMistakes.why: 'Thunder' as a natural phenomenon is uncountable in English (you can talk about 'claps of thunder' to count individual sounds), so avoid making the word itself plural. Use 'a lot of thunder' to describe general amount.

commonMistakes.wrong: The weather was full of rains today.
commonMistakes.right: The weather was rainy today.
commonMistakes.why: 'Rain' describing the weather in general is uncountable, so avoid pluralizing it - instead, consider using the adjective form 'rainy' to describe the day. This is a useful trick: sometimes switching to an adjective avoids the plural noun problem entirely.

commonMistakes.wrong: I heard some good musics at the concert.
commonMistakes.right: I heard some good music at the concert.
commonMistakes.why: 'Music' as an art form is uncountable, so it never takes a plural 's', even when referring to many different songs. Use 'some music' or 'several songs' depending on what you actually mean.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has a lot of homeworks this week.
commonMistakes.right: She has a lot of homework this week.
commonMistakes.why: Even with 'a lot of' (which works for both countable and uncountable nouns), 'homework' itself must remain singular. The quantity word changes ('many' vs 'a lot of'), but the uncountable noun's form never does.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to give you some good newses today.
commonMistakes.right: I want to give you some good news today.
commonMistakes.why: 'News' is uncountable and always singular in English, so it never takes a plural 's', no matter how many separate pieces of news there are. Remember this word specifically: news never becomes 'newses', in any context.

commonMistakes.wrong: There are too much cars on this road.
commonMistakes.right: There are too many cars on this road.
commonMistakes.why: 'Cars' is countable, so it pairs with 'many', not 'much' - this is the same rule as friends/money, just reversed direction again. Whenever the noun is something you could count one by one, use 'many', never 'much'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't have many time today.
commonMistakes.right: I don't have much time today.
commonMistakes.why: 'Time' (in the sense of duration) is uncountable, so it needs 'much', not 'many'. This mistake is extremely common because 'time' can sometimes be countable in other contexts (like 'three times'), which causes confusion.

commonMistakes.wrong: She gave me two pieces of jewelries.
commonMistakes.right: She gave me two pieces of jewelry.
commonMistakes.why: 'Jewelry' is uncountable, so even after 'pieces of' (which already shows quantity), the word itself should stay singular, with no 's' added. Keep the noun in its base uncountable form even when counting phrases are used.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to buy some new furnitures for my office.
commonMistakes.right: I want to buy some new furniture for my office.
commonMistakes.why: This is the same core mistake repeated - 'furniture' simply never takes an 's', no matter the sentence around it. Repetition and practice with this specific word are the best way to make the correct form automatic.

commonMistakes.wrong: There is too much people in this room.
commonMistakes.right: There are too many people in this room.
commonMistakes.why: As before, 'people' is countable plural, so use 'many' and the plural verb 'are', never 'much' and 'is'. This exact sentence pattern is worth memorizing as a fixed correct phrase.

commonMistakes.wrong: I received an useful feedback.
commonMistakes.right: I received useful feedback.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'feedback' is uncountable, it can't take 'a/an' directly - simply drop the article and use the noun alone, or add 'some' before it. Whenever an uncountable noun feels like it needs 'a/an', that's a sign to just remove it.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have less friends than her.
commonMistakes.right: I have fewer friends than her.
commonMistakes.why: This repeats the fewer/less rule - 'friends' is countable, so 'fewer' is correct, not 'less'. Keep testing yourself with this pair of words until it's second nature.

commonMistakes.wrong: There was too much accidents this year.
commonMistakes.right: There were too many accidents this year.
commonMistakes.why: 'Accidents' is countable, so it needs 'many' and the plural verb 'were', not 'much' and 'was'. This mistake often happens because 'much' feels natural before certain nouns out of habit rather than logic.

commonMistakes.wrong: She gave good advices to everyone.
commonMistakes.right: She gave good advice to everyone.
commonMistakes.why: This is the same 'advice' rule appearing once more - it's worth repeating because this is one of the single most common uncountable noun mistakes made by English learners worldwide. Keep 'advice' singular in every context, without exception.

commonMistakes.wrong: I don't have much problems with this.
commonMistakes.right: I don't have many problems with this.
commonMistakes.why: 'Problems' is countable, so 'many' is correct, and 'much' would be wrong here. This is another version of the same recurring pattern - checking whether a noun is countable before choosing 'much' or 'many'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He has few money left.
commonMistakes.right: He has little money left.
commonMistakes.why: Just as 'fewer/less' pairs with countable/uncountable, so do 'few/little' - use 'few' for countable nouns and 'little' for uncountable ones like money. Remember this second pair alongside fewer/less: few + countable, little + uncountable.

commonMistakes.wrong: I only have little friends in this city.
commonMistakes.right: I only have few friends in this city.
commonMistakes.why: This is the reverse case - 'friends' is countable, so it needs 'few', not 'little'. Practicing both money/little and friends/few together will help you keep the pattern straight.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need some informations before I decide.
commonMistakes.right: I need some information before I decide.
commonMistakes.why: This is a final repeat of the most common mistake in this whole list, included again because it's worth truly memorizing: 'information' never, ever takes an 's' in English, no matter the context.

commonMistakes.wrong: We had a beautiful scenery on our trip.
commonMistakes.right: We had beautiful scenery on our trip.
commonMistakes.why: 'Scenery' is uncountable - it describes a whole view or landscape as one general idea, so it never takes 'a/an' in front of it. Simply describe it directly, without an article: 'beautiful scenery', not 'a beautiful scenery'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: I have a lot of furniture.
quiz.answer: I have a lot of furniture.
quiz.explanation: 'Furniture' is uncountable and never takes a plural 's'.
'Advice' is uncountable - use 'a piece of advice' to describe one instance, but never 'advices'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'Friends' is countable, so it pairs with 'many', not 'much'.

quiz.question: I need some ___ about the course. (uncountable noun for facts/details)
quiz.explanation: 'Information' is always singular and uncountable in English.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'People' is countable, so it needs 'fewer' and the plural verb 'are'.
